metadata.name: Software Development Flow
metadata.description: Complete feature development cycle with planning, iterative implementation, numerical checks and validation
metadata.author: mcp-moira-team

nodes.directive:
Study project foundations independently.

{{#neq agent_onboarding_file_path 'skip'}}1) Read the agent onboarding document at {{agent_onboarding_file_path}} — follow ALL rules and constraints defined there
2) Understand how project starts (commands, environment, dependencies) but DO NOT start
3) Do not assume - read files{{else}}No agent onboarding document configured. Study the project using standard approaches:
1) Read README.md and any docs/ directory for project overview
2) Explore directory structure to understand architecture
3) Check package.json / build configs for build system and dependencies
4) Look for existing tests to understand testing approach
5) Do not assume - read files{{/neq}}

Return what you learned about the project.
nodes.completionCondition: Project foundation studied: rules found, architecture understood, build system studied

nodes.directive:
Study implementation details of the project independently.

1) Source code: examine src/, backend/, frontend/, types/, lib/ directories in detail. Understand module structure, key interfaces, and data flow.
2) Tests: study test structure (tests/, __tests__), understand what is tested and how. Note test patterns used.
3) Architecture: understand how components interact, what external dependencies exist, how data flows through the system.
4) Build system: review build configuration (webpack, tsconfig, package.json), understand compilation and bundling.
5) Dependencies: check package.json for key dependencies and their roles.

Focus on understanding HOW the system works, not just WHAT files exist. This knowledge is critical for making correct implementation decisions later.

Return a concise summary of findings for each area.
nodes.completionCondition: Implementation details studied: core architecture understood, key components identified, patterns determined

nodes.directive:
Get specific task requirements. Now need to understand what exactly needs to be done.

1) Ask for clear task description considering architecture
2) Clarify scope and constraints
3) Get specific requirements
4) Evaluate complexity - analyze task complexity (scale 1-10)
5) Remember GitHub issue numbers IF user mentioned them

DO NOT create files - workspace will be created later after feature name confirmation.
nodes.completionCondition: Task defined, scope clarified, complexity evaluated

task_complexity_in_context.description: Task complexity (1-4: express mode, 5-10: full development)

nodes.directive:
Create workspace for developing feature.

1) AUTO-GENERATE feature name from task description:
   - Convert to kebab-case (lowercase, hyphens instead of spaces)
   - Maximum 30 characters
   - Remove special characters
   - Example: "Fix authentication bug" → "fix-authentication-bug"

2) CHECK FOR CONFLICTS:
   - git branch --list "feature/{generated_name}*"
   - ls -d ./moira-ws/{generated_name}-*/
   - If conflict found: add suffix (-2, -3, etc.) to the name
   - DO NOT ask user for confirmation - use generated name directly

3) Generate workspace path with current date/time:
   Format: ./moira-ws/{feature_name}-{YYYYMMDD}-{HHMM}/
   Example: ./moira-ws/fix-auth-bug-20251225-1430/

4) Create workspace folder at generated path

5) Save process ID to file {generated_path}/process-id.txt for recovery after archiving

IMPORTANT: Return workspace_path and feature_name in response - they will be SET as context variables for all subsequent nodes.
nodes.completionCondition: Workspace folder created, process ID saved. Feature name generated.
task-requirements.md created with user task description, complexity, and github issues reference.

nodes.directive:
Present the saved task requirements to the user for confirmation.

1) Read the file {{workspace_path}}/task-requirements.md
2) Present the COMPLETE TEXT to the user without any modifications or summarization
3) After presenting, ask the user: "Did I understand your requirements correctly? Please confirm or tell me what needs to be changed."

Do NOT proceed until the user explicitly confirms or requests changes.
nodes.completionCondition: Full requirements text presented to user. User responded with either confirmation or requested changes.

requirements_confirmed.description: true if user confirmed requirements are correct, false if changes requested

nodes.directive:
The user indicated the requirements need changes.

User feedback: {{user_feedback}}

1) Discuss with the user what exactly needs to be amended
2) Get the corrected/additional requirements
3) Update the file {{workspace_path}}/task-requirements.md with the corrected requirements
4) Make sure ALL original requirements that were NOT changed are preserved

After updating the file, the requirements will be presented to the user again for re-confirmation.
nodes.completionCondition: Requirements file updated with user's corrections. Ready for re-confirmation.

nodes.directive:
Create iterative development plan where each step delivers working functionality. Need to create detailed and executable plan based on deep requirements analysis.

MANDATORY PLAN SECTIONS:

## Problem Statement
What task/problem are we solving? What is not working or what is missing?

## Business Value
Why is this important? What value will this bring?

## Expected Outcome
Expected result. What will work after completion?

## Architecture
Key components and their interaction.

## Steps
(then plan steps)

PLAN CREATION PROCESS:
1) Analyze requirements from task-requirements.md in detail (in context)
2) Study project architecture to understand integration points
3) Analyze task complexity and determine optimal step breakdown
4) Determine dependencies between steps and correct sequence
5) Create plan in {{workspace_path}}/development-plan.md
6) Follow planning requirements: Read planning requirements from file at {{workspace_path}}/planning-standards.md — follow ALL rules defined there.

{{plan_issue_instruction}}

In response indicate structured array of plan steps with detailed tasks, path to plan file for reference, and brief summary of entire plan for notifications.
nodes.completionCondition: Technical plan created with implementation details worked out, integration points, testing strategy

nodes.directive:
This is a normal re-validation loop, expected to converge — NOT a bug and NOT a stuck flow; do NOT report a loop to the user.

Delegate plan review to subagent via Task tool (subagent-review pattern).

IMPORTANT: DO NOT do self-review - your assessment of own work is biased.

1) Launch subagent with prompt:
   YOU ARE plan validator. Your assessment determines if development plan proceeds.

   READ THESE FILES DIRECTLY (do not trust summaries):
   - Plan: {{workspace_path}}/development-plan.md
   - Requirements: {{workspace_path}}/task-requirements.md
   - Planning standards: {{workspace_path}}/planning-standards.md

   VALIDATION CRITERIA:
   1) Step size: not too small (< 1 hour), not too large (> 1 day)
   2) Criteria clarity: each step has verifiable result
   3) Dependencies: steps are in correct order, no circular dependencies
   4) REQUIREMENTS COVERAGE: every requirement from task-requirements.md must be addressed in the plan
   5) Testing: each step includes verification
   6) Architecture: section present and understandable
   7) STANDARDS COMPLIANCE: plan follows rules from planning-standards.md

   RETURN: review_issues_count (number) and issues_found (array of {issue, affected_step, suggested_fix})

2) Honestly pass subagent result:
   - review_issues_count = EXACTLY what subagent found
   - issues_found = EXACT list from subagent
   - DO NOT filter or re-evaluate results

3) Save issues to file:
   - Write ALL issues to {{workspace_path}}/plan-review-issues-{{plan_iteration}}.md
   - Format:
     # Plan Review Issues

     Total issues: [count]

     ## Issue 1: [issue description]
     - Affected step: [step]
     - Suggested fix: [fix]

     ## Issue 2: ...
   - This file will be used by fix step to address EACH issue
nodes.completionCondition: Plan reviewed, all issues found and described (or confirmed no issues)

review_issues_count.description: Number of issues found in plan (0 if no issues)

nodes.directive:
BEFORE fixing: Read planning standards from {{workspace_path}}/planning-standards.md. All fixes MUST comply with these standards.

FIX found issues in plan.

READ issues from {{workspace_path}}/plan-review-issues-{{plan_iteration}}.md

For EACH issue in the file:
1. Identify what needs to be fixed
2. Open plan file: {{workspace_path}}/development-plan.md
3. Apply the fix according to suggested_fix
4. Mark issue as addressed

DO NOT skip any issue. ALL issues from the file must be resolved.

After fixing ALL issues, save updated plan.
Plan will be reviewed again to verify fixes.
nodes.completionCondition: All issues fixed, plan updated

nodes.directive:
Show plan to user for approval.

1) Explain step breakdown logic
2) Show validation strategy for each step
3) Explain why some steps may temporarily break system
4) Ask: "Do you approve the plan for execution? (yes/no)"
5) Get clear yes or no answer

**CRITICALLY IMPORTANT - REACTION TO FEEDBACK:**
- If user said "yes" → plan_approval = "yes"
- If user gave ANY feedback or said "no" → plan_approval = "no"
- DO NOT fix plan yourself!
- Reply plan_approval = "no" and write user_feedback_on_plan with feedback
- Workflow will direct to plan fix branch itself
- All fixes are done only through workflow, not independently

**SAVE FEEDBACK TO FILE:**
If plan_approval = "no", save the user feedback to {{workspace_path}}/user-plan-feedback-{{plan_iteration}}.md before responding. This file will be read by the refinement step.
nodes.completionCondition: Plan presented to user, clear yes or no answer received on plan approval question

plan_approval.description: User plan approval: yes to approve, no for refinement

nodes.directive:
Refine plan - user did not approve it.

CRITICAL: File synchronization is MANDATORY!

1) READ plan change reasons from {{workspace_path}}/plan-change-reasons-{{plan_iteration}}.md
2) Analyze what exactly user is not satisfied with in plan based on the file content
3) FIRST update the file {{workspace_path}}/development-plan.md with refined plan
4) THEN update development_plan variable in context with same data
5) Create summary of changes for re-presentation

Follow planning requirements: Read planning requirements from file at {{workspace_path}}/planning-standards.md — follow ALL rules defined there.

In response:
- Confirm file was updated FIRST (plan_file_updated = "yes")
- Provide updated array of plan steps
- Brief summary of updated plan
- Description of changes made


6) MANDATORY: If plan changes affect requirements, update {{workspace_path}}/task-requirements.md
   - Ensure updated requirements are consistent with the updated plan
   - Do NOT duplicate or create logical conflicts
nodes.completionCondition: Plan refined based on user feedback, development_plan in context updated, ready for re-presentation

nodes.directive:
Present refined plan to user and get confirmation. 1) Show user updated plan 2) Explain what changes were made and why 3) Ask user: "Do you approve the refined plan for execution? (yes)" 4) Get EXPLICIT confirmation.

**CRITICALLY IMPORTANT - REACTION TO FEEDBACK:**
- If user said "yes" → approval = "yes"
- If user gave ANY feedback or said "no" → approval = "no"
- DO NOT fix yourself!
- Reply approval = "no" and write user_feedback with feedback
- Workflow will direct to fix branch itself
- All fixes are done only through workflow, not independently

**SAVE FEEDBACK TO FILE:**
If refinement_approval = "no", save the user feedback to {{workspace_path}}/user-plan-feedback-{{plan_iteration}}.md before responding. This file will be read by the refinement step.
nodes.completionCondition: Refined plan confirmation received from user

nodes.directive:
Implement current plan step completely and with quality. This is key moment - do not proceed further until everything planned is fully completed and system is working. Further checks will help, but main responsibility is on you. Everything depends on quality of your work: 1) Take step from {{workspace_path}}/development-plan.md by index {{current_step_index}} (1-based) 2) Implement all technical tasks from plan without exceptions 3) Do not fear big changes and refactoring - this is normal and necessary 4) If need to rewrite lots of code for proper solution - do it 5) Do not try to quickly work around difficulties - solve them completely 6) Create or modify necessary files 7) Test functionality of new features 8) Ensure system works after your changes. {{code_quality_principles}} In response indicate brief description of implemented functionality.

nodes.directive:
Determine CHANGE TYPE across the ENTIRE current plan step (all iterations combined) and whether UI was affected.

TYPE RULES:

code = ANY changes in application code (src/, backend/, frontend/, packages/, lib/, services/, components/, utils/ etc.). Even one modified code file = code. Code refactoring = code. Bug fixes in code = code. If changed BOTH code AND tests = code.

tests_only = ONLY test files changed (tests/, spec/, __tests__/, *.test.*, *.spec.*), NO application code changed across ALL iterations of this step.

documentation_only = Only content files changed (README, API docs, workflow JSONs, i18n, templates). No code, no tests changed. Plan step has NO test coverage requirement. If plan step has E2E test requirement = code.

research_only = NO project files changed at all, only research and analysis.

ANALYSIS:

1) Determine change type based on all implementation work done on this step (use step-results.md if available in context, otherwise use current session context)
2) When in doubt between code and another type → choose code (safer)
3) Determine has_ui_changes: "yes" if any changes affect UI components, "no" otherwise.
nodes.completionCondition: Change type and UI impact determined for the current plan step

has_ui_changes.description: Whether changes affect UI components (for screenshot validation routing)

nodes.directive: Analyze what changed and what might break. 1) Identify functions you added or modified 2) CRITICAL: Find existing functions that DEPEND on the changed ones (call them, use the same data, rely on their results) 3) Create a COMPLETE list: new + modified + dependent. Return array of all functions to verify.
nodes.completionCondition: CODE ANALYSIS COMPLETED

functionality_list.description: List of functions to verify: new + modified + affected

nodes.directive:
Delegate documentation validation to subagent via Task tool.

**CONTEXT:**
- Documentation standards: {{docs_standards_directive}}
- Workspace: {{workspace_path}}

**SUBAGENT TASK:**
Use Task tool with subagent_type="universal-reviewer" and prompt:

"YOU ARE a documentation validator.

TASK: Verify documentation quality against standards.

READ documentation files in workspace and check against standards. {{docs_standards_directive}}

CHECK:
1. Compliance with documentation standards
2. Technical accuracy - no errors
3. Information correctness
4. No marketing terms or change history
5. Complete and clear

OUTPUT:
- List EVERY issue found with specific location and fix needed
- Count total issues

Return issues_count = 0 if documentation is valid."

**IMPORTANT:** Pass subagent result honestly to output.

Save issues to {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/documentation-issues.md with format:
# Documentation Issues

Total issues: [count]

## Issue 1: [description]
- Standard violated: [which standard]
- Location: [file and section]
- What needs to be fixed: [specific fix]

## Issue 2: ...
nodes.completionCondition: Documentation verified against standards with exact count of issues found

expected_doc_issues.description: Number of documentation issues found that need fixing (0 if documentation is valid)

nodes.directive:
{{docs_standards_directive}}

CRITICAL: FIX AND REDO WHAT WAS INDICATED IN THE CHECK. DON'T REPORT ON WORK - DO THE WORK!

READ issues from {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/documentation-issues.md

Expected issues to fix: {{validate-documentation.expected_doc_issues}}

For EACH issue in the file:
1. Find the documentation that needs fixing (see Location field)
2. Understand what standard was violated
3. Apply the fix according to "What needs to be fixed"
4. Verify fix complies with standard
5. Mark issue as resolved

DO NOT skip any issue. ALL {{validate-documentation.expected_doc_issues}} documentation issues must be fixed.

Count the total number of issues you successfully fixed.
nodes.completionCondition: All documentation issues fixed with exact count of completed fixes

fixed_doc_issues.description: Number of documentation issues successfully fixed

nodes.directive:
CRITICAL: DON'T FIX broken functionality - ONLY VERIFY what works.

Verify each function from functionality_list works. The list contains {{analyze-code-changes.functionality_list.length}} functions - all must work. 1) Test each function: run it, verify it works correctly 2) Count how many functions work correctly 3) For Web UI - use Playwright for browser verification. Indicate how many of {{analyze-code-changes.functionality_list.length}} functions work.
nodes.completionCondition: FUNCTIONALITY VERIFICATION COMPLETED

nodes.directive:
CRITICAL: DON'T FIX failed tests and DON'T FIX code - ONLY RUN tests and COLLECT results.

{{run_tests_directive}}

Run all project tests and get NUMERICAL results:
1) Use the test command from the testing guide
2) Wait for ALL tests to complete
3) Count passed and failed tests

Indicate number of passed and failed tests.
nodes.completionCondition: All tests run, numerical results obtained

nodes.directive:
Analyze failed tests and determine fix strategy.

1) Review failed_tests details
2) Determine failure cause for each test
3) Choose fix strategy:
   - fix_implementation: code works incorrectly (real bug, broken functionality)
   - update_tests: code works correctly, but tests are outdated/need updating

4) Save analysis to {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/test-analysis.md:
   # Test Failure Analysis

   ## Summary
   Total failed: [count]
   Strategy: [fix_implementation or update_tests]

   ## Failed Tests

   ### Test 1: [test name]
   - Error message: [error]
   - Root cause: [analysis of why it fails]
   - Recommended fix: [specific fix to apply]

   ### Test 2: [test name]
   ...

Provide detailed analysis of causes and chosen strategy.
nodes.completionCondition: Test failure causes analyzed and fix strategy determined

fix_strategy.description: fix_implementation if code needs fixing, update_tests if tests need updating

nodes.directive:
{{run_tests_directive}}

CRITICAL: FIX THE CODE - NO REPORTS!

Fix implementation so ALL tests pass.

⚠️ IMPORTANT: ALL failed tests MUST be fixed - even if unrelated to current development stage. Reasons:
- Agent may have accidentally broken existing functionality
- Infrastructure/environment may have changed
- Failed tests block deployment and project development
- Broken tests accumulate quickly and project degrades

READ analysis from {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/test-analysis.md

For EACH failed test in the file:
1. Read the root cause and recommended fix
2. Apply the fix to the code
3. Run the specific test to verify it passes
4. Mark test as fixed

ALL failed tests from the analysis file must be fixed.

{{fix_requirements}}
{{code_quality_principles}}

Confirm CODE IS FIXED and all tests pass.
nodes.completionCondition: APPLICATION CODE FIXED for tests to pass

nodes.directive: Document code fixes. 1) Describe in detail what was fixed 2) Create file {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/test-fixes.md. Return file path.
nodes.completionCondition: Fixes report created

nodes.directive:
{{run_tests_directive}}

Update tests for new functionality.

⚠️ CRITICAL: ALL failed tests MUST be fixed - even if unrelated to current stage. After this workflow will run ALL project tests and won't proceed until ALL pass. Reasons:
- Agent may have accidentally broken other tests during changes
- Failed tests block deployment
- Broken tests accumulate and project degrades

⚠️ TEMPORARY SCRIPTS ARE NOT TESTS:
If you previously created temporary scripts for verification (e.g. in /tmp/, ./temp/, or ad-hoc validators) - this does NOT count as test coverage. These scripts will be deleted and won't remain in project. Tests must be in proper location according to project testing architecture.

You can reuse logic from temporary scripts - just move them to correct location (tests/, __tests__/, spec/ etc. according to project conventions).

1) Review what implementation changes were made
2) Update existing tests and add new tests for new functionality
3) VERIFY updated tests pass BEFORE sending response
4) Save changes description to {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/test-updates.md

{{test_quality_standards}}
{{code_quality_principles}}

Indicate which tests were updated.
nodes.completionCondition: Tests updated for new functionality

{{startup_check_directive}}

nodes.directive:
CRITICAL: DON'T FIX code - ONLY VERIFY compliance with standards.

Check quality and architecture standards.

⚠️ IMPORTANT: If standard is NOT RELEVANT to current changes - consider it MET.
Examples:
- No UI changes → UI/UX patterns = met
- No performance-critical code → Performance = met
- Config only without code → Testability = met
- No new comments needed → Documentation = met

1) Check 10 code quality standards:
   - Typing (TypeScript types without any)
   - Error handling (try/catch, validation)
   - No hacks (no workarounds, temporary solutions)
   - Architectural patterns (compliance with project patterns)
   - Readability (clear names, structure)
   - Testability (code covered by tests)
   - Performance (no performance regression)
   - Security (no security issues)
   - Documentation (comments where needed)
   - No dead code (no unused imports/vars/functions)

2) Check 5 architectural aspects:
   - Architectural integrity (compliance with project architecture)
   - Integration points (proper integration with existing components)
   - Future stages compatibility (doesn't break subsequent plan stages)
   - Error handling (error handling at all levels)
   - UI/UX patterns (if applicable - UI consistency)

3) Count met standards out of 15
4) Count violated standards (actual violations, not counting irrelevant ones)
5) Create BRIEF file {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/code-quality-architecture.md describing ONLY violations:
   - Which standard violated
   - Where problem found (file, component)
   - What needs fixing

Return numbers of met and violated standards.
nodes.completionCondition: STANDARDS COUNT COMPLETED

total_standards_met_count.description: Number of standards met out of 15 (10 quality + 5 architecture)

nodes.directive: CRITICAL: FIX THE CODE - NO REPORTS! Fix quality and architecture problems. 1) Read {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/code-quality-architecture.md 2) Find root cause 3) FIX the problems. {{fix_requirements}} {{code_quality_principles}} Confirm THE FIX.
nodes.completionCondition: QUALITY AND ARCHITECTURE PROBLEMS FIXED

nodes.directive: Document quality and architecture fixes. 1) Describe what was fixed 2) Create file {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/quality-architecture-fixes.md. Return file path.
nodes.completionCondition: Fixes report created

nodes.directive:
Determine how many browser functions require testing after this stage's changes.

1) Read browser/UI rules from {{browser_ui_info_path}}
2) Analyze what changed in this stage — code, API endpoints, UI components
3) List EVERY browser function that could be affected by the changes:
   - Existing functions that might be broken
   - New functions that were added
   - Functions that depend on changed API endpoints or data structures
4) Count the total number of browser functions that need verification
5) Save the function list to {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/browser-functions-expected.md with format:
   # Expected Browser Functions
   Total: [count]
   1. [function name] — [why it needs testing]
   2. [function name] — [why it needs testing]
   ...

If no changes affect browser functionality, set expected_browser_functions = 0.
nodes.completionCondition: Listed all browser functions requiring verification and provided exact count

expected_browser_functions.description: Number of browser functions that require Playwright verification (0 if no browser impact)

nodes.directive:
{{#neq browser_ui_info_path 'skip'}}MANDATORY: Read browser/UI rules from {{browser_ui_info_path}} before validation.{{else}}No browser/UI rules file configured. Use standard browser testing practices for validation.{{/neq}}

1) Read the expected function list from {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/browser-functions-expected.md
2) Start all services required for browser testing
3) Use Playwright to verify EACH function from the expected list
4) For each function record: PASS or FAIL with details
5) Count total number of functions that PASS verification
6) Save results to {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/browser-check.md with format:
   # Browser Verification Results
   Expected: {{check-browser-impact.expected_browser_functions}} | Working: [count]
   ## Results
   1. ✅ [function] — verified working
   2. ❌ [function] — FAILED: [error details]
   ...

{{#neq browser_ui_info_path 'skip'}}Refer to browser UI standards at {{browser_ui_info_path}} for UI testing requirements.{{else}}Apply standard UI testing requirements.{{/neq}}
nodes.completionCondition: All expected browser functions verified via Playwright with exact count of working functions

working_browser_functions.description: Number of browser functions that passed Playwright verification

nodes.directive:
Determine which functions require new tests.

Create a screenshot validation guide for the project.

PURPOSE: This file defines how to create, capture, and validate screenshots of the UI. It enables automated visual verification — instead of manually checking the UI, the agent captures screenshots and creates an HTML report as proof of functionality.

REFERENCE TEMPLATE: The following standards are based on the project's screenshot guide template (dev-flow-fixes/SCREENSHOT-GUIDE-TEMPLATE.md).

EXAMPLE STRUCTURE (adapt to THIS project):

```markdown
# Screenshot Guide

## Overview
Screenshots are used to prove UI functionality works correctly. Each validation creates temporary Playwright scripts that capture the current state of the UI.

## Capture Process
1. Create a temporary Playwright script in a temp directory
2. Script navigates to the target page, performs actions, captures screenshots
3. Screenshots are saved with descriptive names
4. Script is deleted after execution

## Capture Helpers
```typescript
// Helper pattern for screenshot capture
async function captureScreenshot(page, name, options?) {
  await page.screenshot({
    path: `screenshots/${name}.png`,
    fullPage: options?.fullPage ?? false
  });
}
```

## HTML Report
After capturing screenshots, create an HTML report that:
- Embeds all screenshots inline (base64)
- Is self-contained (no external dependencies)
- Shows what was tested and what the result looks like
- Serves as PROOF of functionality — user can approve without manual checking

## Validation Checklist
- [ ] All key pages/views have screenshots
- [ ] Screenshots show expected state (not loading/error)
- [ ] HTML report is self-contained and viewable
- [ ] Report demonstrates the functionality works
```

test_quality_standards.default: QUALITY TESTING STANDARDS: Test reality: DO NOT write tests for the sake of tests, verify real use cases and user scenarios, avoid synthetic tests without practical value. Project structure: place tests in folders according to project architecture, follow naming patterns adopted in the project, use existing test utilities and helpers, follow test environment configuration. Reality coverage: cover ALL new functionality without gaps, test edge cases and error handling, cover integration points with other components. Isolation and independence: tests are independent of each other, do not affect execution order, use proper setup/teardown, clean up state after themselves. Readability and maintenance: clear test names describing what is being tested, clear test structure with arrange/act/assert, meaningful assertion messages, easy to update when code changes. Efficiency: tests run quickly without performance regression, optimized for CI/CD pipeline, use functional snapshots if framework supports for easier maintenance and updates. Project integration: use test run commands adopted in the project, follow project conventions, do not duplicate existing tests.

fix_requirements.default: FIX REQUIREMENTS: ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS - find the root cause of the problem, do not fix symptoms, understand why the problem occurred. COMPLETE FIX - fix ALL problems completely without exceptions, superficial fixes DO NOT work and will be detected by the next check, do not leave unfinished work for later. MANDATORY SELF-CHECK BEFORE COMPLETION - VERIFY that the fix works through actual execution, RUN tests to validate the fix, ENSURE nothing is broken in existing functionality, TEST all affected areas, DO NOT proceed to execute_step without verifying the fix works. NO SHORTCUTS - do not look for workarounds and quick solutions, if major changes or global refactoring are needed - do them without hesitation, rewriting significant portions of code may be required - this is normal and correct. CYCLE ESCAPE STRATEGY - workflow checks will be executed anyway and will detect problems, the only way to escape the fix cycle is to do it right the first time, self-validation BEFORE submitting to workflow saves time and iterations, validate through running tests BEFORE claiming fixed.

planning_requirements.default:
# PLANNING REQUIREMENTS

## CRITICAL RULES (READ FIRST)

### FORBIDDEN STEP TYPES - REJECT PLAN IF CONTAINS:
- "Testing" / "Write tests" / "Add tests" as separate step
- "Final validation" / "Final check" / "Verification"
- "E2E testing" / "Integration testing" as separate step
- "Production deployment" / "Deploy and verify"
- "Documentation" as separate step (unless task IS documentation)
- "Cleanup" / "Code cleanup" as separate step (unless task IS cleanup/refactoring)
- ANY step that ONLY tests/validates without implementing NEW functionality or refactoring

### WHY TESTS MUST BE BUILT-IN (NOT SEPARATE):
- Separate test step = admission that previous step is incomplete
- Gate validation requires working tests to verify step completion
- "Write tests" as step means agent shipped untested code
- Test proves step is complete - without tests, step is unverified

### EACH STEP MUST:
- Implement new functionality OR refactor existing code
- Include tests for that functionality (built-in, not separate)
- Include self-verification (built-in, not separate)
- Leave code clean (no separate cleanup step needed)

### BAD EXAMPLE (FORBIDDEN):
```
Step 1: Implement feature X
Step 2: Write tests for feature X    ← FORBIDDEN: testing-only
Step 3: Final validation             ← FORBIDDEN: validation-only
```

### BAD PLAN (REAL EXAMPLE - DO NOT COPY):
```
Step 1: Database layer for user isolation
Step 2: API endpoints for workflows
Step 3: E2E and Production             ← FORBIDDEN: testing-only step
Step 4: Documentation and cleanup      ← FORBIDDEN: doc-only step
```

### GOOD EXAMPLE (CORRECT):
```
Step 1: Implement feature X with unit tests
        Verify: feature works, tests pass

Step 2: Implement feature Y with integration tests
        Verify: X+Y work together, all tests pass
```

### CORRECT VERSION OF BAD PLAN:
```
Step 1: Database layer with unit tests
        Verify: queries isolated per user, tests pass

Step 2: API endpoints with integration tests
        Verify: auth works, E2E passing, all tests pass
```

---

## STEP SIZE - FUNCTIONAL BLOCKS

Step = completed functional block with verifiable result.

NOT too small: "create a file", "add import"
NOT too large: "implement entire frontend"

CORRECT granularity:
- Database layer with user isolation
- MCP integration with authentication
- Web UI for workflow management

WHY: Workflow has gate validation after each step - expensive in time.
- Too small steps = dozens of checks instead of 3-5
- Too large steps = agent gets lost

---

## STEP FEASIBILITY CHECK

Each step must be:
- Absolutely executable by agent
- Maximally specific in functionality
- Have clear completion criterion

**HUGE STEP = RED FLAG** of task misunderstanding.
Actions: STOP → study project → ask user → web search → delegate analysis.

**LARGE STEP = PLANNING FAILURE** - split it.

---

## ARCHITECTURE AT PLAN START

MANDATORY Architecture section after Goal:
- Overall solution architecture
- Key components and interaction
- Architectural decisions (technologies, approaches)
- Diagrams if applicable (ASCII, Mermaid, text)

Not too much - conceptual understanding is enough.

Plan undergoes mandatory validation - user confirms architecture before execution.

---

## FORBIDDEN IN PLAN - SPECIFIC FILES AND CODE

FORBIDDEN:
- Specific file paths
- Function/method names
- Line numbers
- Code examples (TypeScript, SQL, React)
- "Files Created/Modified" lists

CORRECT:
- "Implement authentication layer"
- "Update database schema for user isolation"
- "Create UI components for workflow display"

WHY: Agent must ITSELF analyze code and find where to implement.
Plan with files creates false sense of completeness - agent forgets critical details.

---

## WHAT TO WRITE IN STEPS

INCLUDE:
- Functionality and behavior description
- Integration points (conceptually)
- Expected result after step
- Verification criteria (what should work + tests pass)

EXAMPLE:
```
## Step 2: API layer for user workflows

Implement REST endpoints for workflow CRUD operations with integration tests covering auth scenarios.
Endpoints use authenticated context from Step 1.
Include request validation and error handling.

Verify: API returns user's workflows only, rejects unauthorized access. All integration tests pass.
```

---

## PLAN FOR REVIEWER WITHOUT CONTEXT

Plan must be detailed enough that reviewer WITHOUT conversation history can:
- Fully understand what each step accomplishes
- Verify work is complete

BUT: Detail ≠ code and files. Detail = clear FUNCTIONALITY and RESULT description.

Reviewer runs WITHOUT CONTEXT - this is architectural system decision.

---

## GRADUAL BUILDUP (LAYER ORDER)

1) Base layer: data models, core logic, services + unit tests
2) Integration layer: API endpoints, MCP tools + integration tests
3) UI layer: Web interface, forms + browser tests (Playwright)

FROM CORE TO INTERFACE. Never start with UI.

---

## PLAN FORMAT

Structure:
```
# Title
## Goal (one sentence)
## Architecture (components, decisions)
## Step 1: [name] with [test type] tests. Verify: [criteria], tests pass.
## Step 2: [name] with [test type] tests. Verify: [criteria], tests pass.
...
```

FORBIDDEN sections:
- Overview, Summary
- Acceptance Criteria (separate)
- Validation blocks with bash commands
- Files Created/Modified
- UPDATED REQUIREMENTS
- Testing (as separate section)
- Final validation

---

## PLAN VOLUME

- Tiny task: 1 step is NORMAL and CORRECT
- Simple task: 50-100 lines, 2-4 steps
- Medium task: 100-200 lines, 5-7 steps
- Complex task: 200-300 lines, 8-12 steps

**ONE STEP IS OK**: If task is small - plan with 1 step is correct.
DO NOT artificially inflate plan to have more steps.
DO NOT split simple task into multiple steps just to "look thorough".

If >400 lines - you are writing code instead of plan.

---

## NO SUBITEMS

Plan = simple list of steps: 1, 2, 3, 4

FORBIDDEN: 5.1, 5.2, 5.3 subitems

Subitems = sign that step is too large → split into separate steps.

---

## USER APPROVAL STEPS

Some steps require user confirmation:
- UI changes (visual)
- Breaking changes
- Architectural decisions
- API changes

Mark in plan: `[USER APPROVAL]` before continuing.

---

## PLANNING PRINCIPLES

- CODE RESEARCH for existing patterns
- WEB RESEARCH for best practices
- LIBRARY DOCUMENTATION for APIs
- DO NOT ASSUME - only verified facts
- CURRENT library versions

Good plan = result of deep analysis, not guess.

---

## FORMULA

```
Plan = Architecture + Functional blocks (1-12 steps) + Verification criteria
Each step: functionality + tests + verification (ALL BUILT-IN)
WITHOUT: code, files, validation blocks, subitems, testing-only steps, cleanup steps
```

Good plan reads in 2 minutes and gives complete understanding.

9. ATOMICITY & REDUNDANCY (no global scope):
- An agent often receives ONE plan item's directive in isolation — other items and general rules are NOT in its context.
- Each item is fully self-contained: restate every nuance it needs, repeat the relevant original requirements INSIDE the item, and DUPLICATE cross-cutting actions (progress report, run tests, commit) into EVERY item.
- There is no shared/global scope — redundancy across items is REQUIRED, not a smell.

code_quality_principles.default: === AGENT IDENTITY PROTOCOL === YOU ARE AN AI AGENT, NOT A HUMAN: You have NO fatigue, no time constraints, no desire to avoid difficulties. DO NOT IMITATE human weaknesses and limitations. TAKE THE BEST ENGINEER PATTERNS: full responsibility, deep understanding, completion without compromises. You can work with ANY complexity without limitations. SOLVE AT THE ROOT, do not work around problems due to perceived complexity. === WORKFLOW BLOCKING PROTOCOL === CRITICALLY IMPORTANT: DO NOT proceed to execute_step until ALL tasks are 100% COMPLETE. Workflow is BLOCKED until full completion. Any attempt to proceed with incomplete work - PROTOCOL VIOLATION. === TASK COMPLETION REQUIREMENTS === ANTI-AVOIDANCE RULES: FORBIDDEN to claim main goal achieved without 100% completion, use significant improvement to mask incompleteness, say comprehensive work ongoing instead of completing tasks, move to next task with current incomplete, avoid difficult parts of task. MANDATORY to complete ALL subtasks before claiming completion, provide specific numerical evidence for each claim, honestly report remaining problems with exact numbers, fix ALL identified problems before continuing, fulfill all requirements with tool-based verification. === BIG TASK PROTOCOL === DO NOT FEAR BIG TASKS! If task seems huge - this is NORMAL and EXPECTED: Break into subtasks but COMPLETE ALL without exceptions. DO NOT SKIP difficult parts for speed. DO NOT look for shortcuts and workarounds. Large changes and refactoring are NECESSARY for quality. Better to rewrite lots of code correctly than make hacks. Complexity is NOT an excuse for incompleteness. === EVIDENCE PROTOCOL === COMPLETION EVIDENCE IS MANDATORY: Every completed must have tool-based verification. Every works correctly must have actual test execution. Every significantly improved must have exact before/after numbers. Every transition to execute_step must have completion verification. === QUALITY STANDARDS === TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS: RESPONSIBILITY: You are experienced engineer - full responsibility for result. EVERYTHING depends on your work. QUALITY: Code must be clean, without issues, with proper typing. QUALITY CODE: No hacks, workarounds, temporary solutions - do it right immediately. PROBLEMS: Solve problems completely, do not work around them. ERRORS: Analyze causes, fix root of problem, not symptoms. SIMPLICITY: First look for simple solutions, then complicate if needed. CHECKS: After every significant change - testing and validation. DEAD CODE: Remove all unused, maintain cleanliness. RESULT: Focus on working solution, not on process for process sake. BIG CHANGES: DO NOT fear big changes and refactoring - this is NORMAL and NECESSARY for quality. Better to rewrite lots of code correctly than make hacks. DO NOT SLACK: DO NOT try to quickly do something instead of proper solution. Flow checks are help, NOT replacement for your responsibility. ADDITIONAL DEVELOPMENT PRINCIPLES: CODE STUDY: DO NOT hesitate to study existing code for understanding patterns and integration points. WEB RESEARCH: Use internet to study best practices and solve problems. CURRENT VERSIONS: Use latest stable library versions, read their documentation. DO NOT ASSUME: If something does not work - read documentation, do not invent solutions. DOCUMENTATION: For library problems - official documentation first, then search. === CODEBASE MAINTENANCE PROTOCOL === RESPONSIBILITY FOR ENTIRE CODEBASE: CANNOT PASS BY PROBLEMS: If you see non-compliance with standards, issues, hacks, lints, warnings in EXISTING code you touch - FIX immediately. BROKEN TESTS: See failing tests, .skip, .only in project - FIX or delete, DO NOT write new tests leaving old ones broken. FIX ON TOUCH: TODO, FIXME, console.log, commented code in files you edit - remove immediately. IMPROVE CODE: DO NOT leave code worse than you found - improve quality on every touch. FULL RESPONSIBILITY: You are responsible for ENTIRE project, not just new code - maintain entire codebase at high level, you will be working with it. === SELF-MONITORING PROTOCOL === CRITICAL QUESTIONS BEFORE EACH execute_step: Can I prove this claim with tool output? Would this pass code review by human without access to my work? Am I avoiding something difficult here? Is this really finished or am I just tired of working on it? Do I have specific evidence for each claim? Are ALL requirements 100% fulfilled or am I masking incompleteness?
